risk_notes: No external dependencies declared or referenced; the implementation relies entirely on native Web APIs (fetch, Promise, setTimeout) with no package manager manifests present in the agent output.
No package.json, requirements.txt, or other dependency manifest is present, so no pinned or unpinned third-party packages can be assessed for CVEs.

The code uses the native fetch API without retry logic, circuit breaking, timeout handling, or error classification — contrast with zero-dependency libraries such as fetch-smartly (github.com/Ali-Raza-Arain/fetch-smartly), fetch-backoff (github.com/faizkhairi/fetch-backoff), ffetch (github.com/fetch-kit/ffetch), and @zayne-labs/callapi (github.com/zayne-labs/callapi) which provide these features while also remaining dependency-free.
No timeout or AbortSignal is applied to any fetch call; a slow or hung server will cause requests to hang indefinitely, which is a reliability and potential resource-exhaustion risk.
No HTTP error status checking is performed (response.ok is never tested); 4xx and 5xx responses are silently parsed as JSON and returned as if successful, which can mask server-side errors.
No retry or backoff logic is present; transient network failures or rate-limit responses (429, 503, etc.) will surface as unhandled rejections to the caller.
No input validation or sanitisation is applied to baseURL or endpoint parameters; a caller supplying a malicious or malformed URL could trigger unexpected network requests.
No Content-Type validation is performed on responses before calling .json(); non-JSON responses will throw a parse error that propagates as an unhandled rejection.
The rate-limiting queue uses a fire-and-forget catch (() => {}) pattern that silently swallows errors from queued tasks, making debugging difficult.

findings.evidence: No package.json, requirements.txt, or other dependency manifest is present, so no pinned or unpinned third-party packages can be assessed for CVEs.
